summary: On the morning of September 11, 2001, I was at Arlington House, preparing to open at 9:30. I was scheduled to be on the second floor, and shortly after arriving, I heard about the World Trade Center being hit. Later, I felt the boom from the plane hitting the Pentagon and saw smoke from the front of Arlington House. We had to close the house and conduct a security sweep, which was nerve-wracking given the uncertainty of the situation.

I first heard about the World Trade Center being hit around 9:25 a.m. on September 11, 2001, when Jan, our gardener, rushed in with the news. At first, I thought it was a horrible accident, not realizing there were two towers or the scale of the attack. Later, as we watched the news on a small TV, we saw repeated footage of the planes hitting the towers, which was chilling and surreal.

On September 11, 2001, while at Arlington House, I heard and felt a big boom which I later learned was the plane hitting the Pentagon. We saw smoke from the direction of the Pentagon, and it became clear that something serious was happening. The proximity of the Pentagon to Arlington made the event feel very immediate and personal, and it was a source of concern for all of us that day.

Linda Kacmarcik was with us at Arlington House on the morning of September 11, 2001. She called me down from the second floor to discuss whether to open the museum building, showing her involvement in operations. Despite the chaos, Linda remained calm and composed, which contrasted with others like Karen who were visibly upset. Linda's steady demeanor helped us manage the situation as best we could.

Frank Cucurullo was the supervisor who arrived at Arlington House shortly after the attacks on September 11, 2001. He organized a security sweep of the house with several of us, including myself, Linda, and Malcolm. Frank was trying to figure out how to respond to unprecedented events, considering the possibility that our site could be a target. He also coordinated who would stay behind while others went home.

Joy Kinard was present with us at Arlington House during the events of September 11, 2001. She was part of the small group inside the house when we conducted the security sweep after the attacks. Although I don’t recall many details about her actions, she was one of the team members helping to check the premises during that tense morning.

Malcolm Willoughby was our maintenance supervisor at Arlington House on September 11, 2001. He participated in the security sweep with Frank, myself, and others, checking bathrooms and other areas for anything suspicious. Malcolm had a small radio with him which we used to listen to reports, including one about an unidentified plane approaching. Later, we gathered in his office around a small TV to watch the news coverage.

Keith Drews was part of our team at Arlington House on September 11, 2001, and he volunteered to stay behind after the security sweep while the rest of us left. He didn’t have a car, which concerned me given the uncertain transportation situation, but he insisted on staying. His willingness to remain helped us manage the site during a very uncertain time.

On the morning of September 11, 2001, while at Arlington House, we heard a loud boom that was later explained as the sonic boom of jets taking off from Langley. These jets were likely scrambling to intercept the plane that hit the Pentagon or responding to reports of an unidentified plane nearby. The mention of Langley brought home the military response happening close to us.

Karen was with us at Arlington House on September 11, 2001, and I remember she was visibly upset and crying while we watched the news coverage. Her emotional reaction stood out, especially compared to others like Linda who remained more composed. Seeing Karen's response helped me understand how deeply the events were affecting people around me.

During the September 11 attacks, I was near D.C., specifically at Arlington House, and we closely followed the unfolding events in the city. The Pentagon attack was particularly alarming given its proximity to D.C., and later that day, I drove into the city to pick up a friend. The city felt surreal and deserted, with National Guard troops visible and a tense atmosphere that felt like an under-siege situation.

I grew up in Chantilly, Virginia, and on September 11, 2001, after leaving Arlington House, I returned to my family home there. It was about thirty miles outside the city, and I ended up hosting friends who couldn’t get home due to disrupted transportation. My mom made us dinner, and we spent the afternoon watching the news, which was a small comfort amid the chaos.

Rosslyn was where one of my friends worked on September 11, 2001. She had just moved to the area from Charlotte, North Carolina, and I called her to offer a ride since I had my car. The traffic was heavy, and it took much longer than usual to get to her office, but I was glad to help her and her coworker get out of the city safely.

One of my close friends who I went to school with in North Carolina is originally from Charlotte. She had just moved to the D.C. area the week before September 11, 2001. I picked her up from her office in Rosslyn that day to help her get out of the city safely amid the chaos.

After leaving Arlington House on September 11, 2001, I drove onto Route 50, which was packed with traffic and servicemen and women walking along the road. What should have been a five-minute drive took about twenty-five minutes due to the congestion and the tense atmosphere. Route 50 was a key route out of the city that day, but it was heavily affected by the emergency response.

Alexandria, Virginia, was where one of my friends, Scott, lived. On September 11, 2001, he was trying to get home from downtown D.C. but ended up going the wrong way on the metro due to disruptions. We convinced him to continue toward Vienna, where I picked him up so he could stay at my house in Chantilly until things settled down.

Vienna, Virginia, was the metro station where I picked up my friend Scott on September 11, 2001, after he was unable to get home due to metro disruptions. We decided it was safer for him to stay with us in Chantilly for the time being. Vienna became a key meeting point for us during the chaotic transportation situation that day.

While the Fourteenth Street Bridge is mentioned in the context of my bus route and commuting, on September 11, 2001, I was not directly on it but aware of the general disruption in the area. The bridge is part of the routes connecting D.C. and Virginia, and the attacks caused major transportation issues around there. It was part of the broader network affected by the emergency that day.

On September 11, 2001, while driving out of the city on Route 50, I saw many servicemen and women in uniform walking down the middle of the road. It was clear that the National Guard had been deployed as part of the emergency response. Their presence contributed to the feeling that the city was under siege and added to the surreal atmosphere of that day.

After the attacks on September 11, 2001, we tried to get a late edition of The Washington Post to understand what was happening. As a historian interested in memory, I wanted to have something tangible from that day. The newspaper was part of how we tried to process and document the events as they unfolded.

I experienced the September 11 attacks firsthand while working at Arlington House. The day started with news of the World Trade Center being hit, followed by the Pentagon attack, which I felt as a boom. We conducted security sweeps, helped friends stranded by transportation shutdowns, and watched the tragic events unfold on TV. The entire day was surreal, filled with fear, confusion, and a strong sense of community as we supported each other.
